s: bleiben means 'to stay, remain' in German
e: Strong verb in German. Conjugates as: bleiben → ba... in past tense. Common in everyday speech.
Cardinal number. Used for counting and expressing quantity. Forms the basis for ordinals and other number-based expressions.
Preposition indicating spatial relationship. Requires specific case (accusative or dative). Used to describe location and direction.
Nein is the most common way to express negation or refusal in German. Unlike 'nicht' (not), which modifies verbs or adjectives, nein stands alone as a complete response.
Weit describes both physical distance (far away) and manner (widely spread). It's an adjective/adverb that often appears in comparisons: 'weit weg' (far away), 'weit offen' (wide open).
Heute is the most common way to reference the current day. Related words: 'heutig' (today's), 'heutzutage' (nowadays, these days).
Hoch is a key adjective describing vertical elevation. Related: 'höher' (higher), 'Hochhaus' (high-rise building), 'Hochwasser' (flood—literally 'high water').
Welch is an interrogative pronoun used for asking questions. It changes form based on gender and case: 'welcher' (m.), 'welche' (f.), 'welches' (n.). Unlike English, German distinguishes these.

s: erst means 'first, only, not until' in German
e: Erst has multiple meanings depending on context. As an adverb: 'erst heute' (only today). The famous saying 'Erst die Arbeit, dann das Vergnügen' (first work, then pleasure) shows its temporal meaning.
Einmal literally breaks down as 'ein' (one) + 'mal' (time). Beyond 'once,' it's used in 'einmal mehr' (one more time) and 'was ist einmal, ist einmal' (what's done is done).
Nehmen is one of the most common German verbs. Conjugation example: ich nehme, du nimmst, er nimmt. Related words: 'Annahme' (acceptance), 'Nebeneffekt' (side effect, literally 'taken-along effect').

Ob is a subordinating conjunction introducing indirect questions. 'Er weiß nicht, ob es möglich sein wird' (He doesn't know whether it will be possible). It's distinct from 'wenn' (if/when) in conditional clauses.

s: dabei means 'with it, there' in German (da-pronoun)
e: 'Dabei' combines 'da' (there) + 'bei' (at/with). It's a da-pronoun used to reference something previously mentioned: 'Ich möchte gern dabei sein' (I'd like to be in on it). Can also mean 'in the process of doing something.'
Beide is an indefinite pronoun meaning both people or things. It changes with case: 'Beide sind klug' (Both are smart). Feminine form 'Beides' can mean 'both (things)' in the neuter sense.
'Eben' has dual meanings: 1) An adverb meaning 'just now' or 'a moment ago' ('Ich habe eben eine E-Mail bekommen'). 2) An adjective meaning 'flat' or 'level.' Context determines the meaning.

q: What does 'der Mann' mean in English?
e: Mann (with the definite article 'der') is a masculine noun. Note: 'mann' in compounds like 'Schneemann' (snowman) or 'Feuerwehrmann' (firefighter) adds the occupational or descriptive sense.

s: erst mal means 'first time' in German
q: What does 'erst mal' mean in English?
e: 'Erst mal' combines 'erst' (first) + 'mal' (time). It's a softer way to say 'first of all' or 'to begin with.' Often used to postpone decisions: 'Darüber muss ich erst mal nachdenken' (I need to think about it first).

s: heißen means 'to be called' in German
a: to be called, to be named
e: Heißen is the verb for a person's or thing's name. Conjugation: 'Ich heiße...' (My name is...), 'Sie heißt Anja' (She is called Anja). Also means 'to mean' or 'to signify' in some contexts.
'Ohne...zu' is a special infinitive construction. Example: 'Er geht, ohne etwas zu sagen' (He goes without saying anything). The 'zu' + infinitive shows the action not being done.

s: natürlich means 'naturally, of course' or 'natural' in German
e: 'Natürlich' as an adverb means 'of course' or 'naturally.' As an adjective: 'natürliche Vitamine' (natural vitamins). The root is 'Natur' (nature).
Ohne is a preposition taking the accusative case. Examples: 'ohne meine Handtasche' (without my purse), 'ohne ihn' (without him). It's fundamental to expressing absence or exclusion.

s: einfach means 'simple, easy' or 'simply' in German
e: 'Einfach' as an adjective means 'simple' or 'easy.' As an adverb: 'einfach keine Lust' (simply no desire). It's one of the most versatile descriptive words in German.

s: liegen means 'to lie' in German (as in reclining)
e: Liegen means to be in a lying/horizontal position or to be located somewhere. Conjugation: 'Der Kranke liegt im Bett' (The patient lies in bed). Not to be confused with 'lügen' (to lie/deceive).
Dort is a directional adverb pointing to a specific location. Shorter form: 'da' (there/here). 'Dort drüben' (over there) is a common phrase. Dort emphasizes distance more than 'da.'

'Vielleicht' literally means 'perhaps' or 'maybe'—expressing uncertainty. It's commonly used to soften statements: 'Vielleicht hast du Recht' (Maybe you're right). The root is 'viel' (much) + 'leicht' (light).

s: das heißt (d.h.) means 'that is' in German (i.e.)
q: What does 'das heißt (d.h.)' mean in English?
e: 'Das heißt' (literally 'that is called') is the German equivalent of 'i.e.' or 'that is.' The abbreviation 'd.h.' is used in writing. It introduces clarification or rephrasing.

Denken is a fundamental verb meaning 'to think.' Conjugation: 'ich denke, du denkst, er denkt.' Related: 'Andenken' (souvenir/memento), 'Gedanke' (thought). Often paired with prepositions: 'denken an' (think of).

s: so genannt means 'so called' in German
q: What does 'so genannt' mean in English?
e: 'So genannt' (often written 'so genannte' in attributive form) means 'so-called' or 'alleged.' It's used to introduce something by its accepted name, often with a hint of skepticism or irony.

q: What does 'das Haus' mean in English?
e: Haus (with definite article 'das') is a neuter noun. It refers to a building where people live. Related words: 'Haushalt' (household), 'Hausfrau' (housewife), 'Hausaufgaben' (homework—literally 'house assignments').

s: die Frage nach means 'the question of' in German
q: What does 'die Frage nach' mean in English?
d: to force one's way in, penetrate
e: 'Die Frage nach' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'the question about/of.' When asking about something, Germans use this construction: 'Frage nach dem Preis' (question about price). The noun 'Frage' is feminine.

s: halten means 'to stop, hold' in German
e: Halten is a key verb with multiple meanings: to hold ('Er hält eine Blume'), to stop ('Der Bus hält hier'), to keep ('etwas halten'). Related: 'Haltung' (posture/attitude), 'erhalten' (to receive).
'Möglichst' (derived from 'möglich'=possible) means 'as...as possible.' Examples: 'möglichst bald' (as soon as possible), 'möglichst gut' (as well as possible). It expresses an optimal goal.

s: gleich means 'same, right away, just' in German
e: 'Gleich' as an adjective means 'same' ('die gleiche Meinung'=same opinion). As an adverb, it means 'right away' or 'in a moment.' 'Gleichzeitig' (simultaneous) combines 'gleich' + 'Zeit' (time).
'Möglich' is an adjective meaning 'possible.' Examples: 'Das ist möglich' (That's possible), 'unmöglich' (impossible). Related: 'Möglichkeit' (possibility), 'möglichst' (as...as possible).

q: What does 'die Frage' mean in English?
e: Frage is a feminine noun meaning 'question.' Plural: 'Fragen.' Verb: 'fragen' (to ask). Compounds: 'Fragebogen' (questionnaire), 'Fragemensch' (inquisitive person).

s: nennen means 'to name, call' in German
e: Nennen means 'to name' or 'to call (someone something).' Example: 'Sie nennen ihren Sohn Max' (They call their son Max). Related: 'Beiname' (nickname), 'Namenszug' (monogram). Unlike 'heißen,' nennen is transitive.

Tun is an irregular verb meaning 'to do' or 'to act.' Conjugation: 'ich tue, du tust, er tut.' It's often used colloquially instead of specific verbs: 'Was tust du?' (What are you doing?). Related: 'Tat' (deed).

s: sprechen means 'to speak, talk' in German
e: Sprechen is a strong verb meaning 'to speak' or 'to talk.' Conjugation: 'ich spreche, du sprichst, er spricht.' Compounds: 'Gespräch' (conversation), 'Sprache' (language), 'Sprecher' (speaker).

q: What does 'das Land' mean in English?
e: Land (neuter noun) has dual meanings: 'country' (in a political sense) and 'land' (as in countryside). Example: 'Dänemark ist ein kleines Land' (Denmark is a small country). Plural: 'Länder' (countries/states).

'Seit' is a preposition taking the dative case, expressing time. 'Seit vier Jahren' (for four years), 'seit Montag' (since Monday). Note: German uses present tense with 'seit' where English uses present perfect.

Glauben means 'to believe' in both religious ('an Gott glauben'=believe in God) and secular contexts ('Ich glaube dir'=I believe you). Related: 'Glaube' (faith/belief), 'Gläubiger' (believer/creditor).

s: während means 'during' or 'while, whereas' in German
e: 'Während' as a preposition means 'during' (takes dative). As a conjunction, it means 'while' or 'whereas.' Examples: 'Während der Ferien' (during vacation), 'Während du liest...' (While you're reading...).
'Führen' means 'to lead' (as in guiding someone) or 'to conduct.' Example: 'Die Straße führt ins Dorf' (The road leads to the village). Related: 'Führer' (leader), 'Führerschein' (driver's license).

s: dazu means 'in addition, to that' in German (da-pronoun)
e: 'Dazu' combines 'da' (there/to that) + 'zu' (to). It's a da-pronoun used to express: 'What do you say about that?' ('Was sagt ihr dazu?'). Also means 'for that purpose' or 'with it.'

'Solch' is an indefinite pronoun meaning 'such.' Forms: 'solcher' (m.), 'solche' (f.), 'solches' (n.). Example: 'Solche Pflanzen sind selten' (Such plants are rare). Can also function as an adjective: 'ein solcher Mann' (such a man).

s: auf die Frage means 'to the question' in German
q: What does 'auf die Frage' mean in English?
e: 'Auf die Frage' (lit. 'to/on the question') is a preposition + article construction answering or responding to something asked. Example: 'Auf die Frage gab es nur negative Antworten' (To the question, there were only negative answers).
Zeigen means 'to show' or 'to point out.' Examples: 'Der Führer zeigt die Stadt' (The guide shows the city), 'zeig mir!' (Show me!). Related: 'Zeichen' (sign), 'Zeiger' (pointer/hand on a clock).

Mögen is both a modal verb (may/might) and a main verb meaning 'to like.' Example: 'Ich mag Himbeeren' (I like raspberries). Conjugation: 'ich mag, du magst, er mag.' Related: 'möglich' (possible), 'Macht' (power).

'Gar' is an adverb intensifying negation. 'Gar nicht' (not at all), 'gar nichts' (nothing at all). Older meaning: 'cooked' or 'done.' Example: 'Ich gehöre dir ganz und gar' (I belong to you completely and entirely).

s: gar nicht means 'not at all' in German
q: What does 'gar nicht' mean in English?
e: 'Gar nicht' is a strong negation combining 'gar' (at all) + 'nicht' (not). Example: 'Das stimmt gar nicht' (That's not true at all). Stronger than simple 'nicht'—expresses emphatic disagreement.

s: dürfen means 'to be allowed to, may' in German
e: Dürfen is a modal verb expressing permission or possibility. Conjugation: 'ich darf, du darfst, er darf.' Example: 'Darf ich noch ein Eis essen?' (May I have another ice cream?). Related: 'Dauer' (duration).

'Letzt' is an adjective meaning 'last.' Forms: 'der letzte' (m.), 'die letzte' (f.), 'das letzte' (n.). Examples: 'Pauline kam als Letzte ins Ziel' (Pauline came in last), 'am letzten Tag' (on the last day).
'Spät' is an adjective/adverb meaning 'late.' Examples: 'Es ist schon spät' (It's already late), 'zu spät' (too late). Comparative: 'später' (later), superlative: 'am spätesten' (latest).
